data.title: Savara GAAP EPS of -$0.13
data.description:
• Savara press release (SVRA): Q4 GAAP EPS of -$0.13.

  • Research and development expenses for the fourth quarter of 2025 and 2024 were $20.9 million and $23.3 million, respectively.

    General and administrative expenses for the fourth quarter of 2025 and 2024 were $12.5 million and $7.8 million, respectively.

    As of December 31, 2025, the Company had cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments of $235.7 million.

    Fiscal Year 2025 Financial Results

    The Company’s net loss for the year ended December 31, 2025 was $118.8 million, or $(0.53) per share, compared with a net loss of was $95.9 million, or $(0.48) per share for the year ended December 31, 2024.

    Research and development expenses increased $3.4 million, or 4.3%, to $81.4 million for the year ended December 31, 2025 from $78.0 million for the year ended December 31, 2024. This increase was primarily due to the performance of tasks related to our MOLBREEVI program, which includes $5.7 million of costs related to regulatory affairs and quality assurance, primarily driven by the BLA submission; $0.5 million of costs related to our chemistry, manufacturing, and controls activities; and $1.0 million other departmental overhead, partially offset by a decrease of $3.8 million in clinical costs.

    General and administrative expenses increased $17.0 million, or 68.0%, to $42.1 million for the year ended December 31, 2025 from $25.0 million for the year ended December 31, 2024.

data.title: AMD ownership increases, Synopsys sees a decrease as fund managers alter portfolios
data.description:
The semiconductor industry has received notable attention from investors, given how much of the artificial intelligence spending boom has benefited the industry. And Bank of America pointed to recent data from active fund managers making changes in their portfolios as worthy of attention.

According to the investment firm's February Equity Strategy data, the relative weighting for the industry continued to decline among active managers, down to 0.84x, the lowest since June 2012, and down from 0.89x in January. 

Despite the declines in relative weighting, the compute area of semiconductors has seen the greatest increase in ownership, led by AMD (AMD). Fund managers increased their position in AMD by 1,001 basis points on a quarter-over-quarter basis, Bank of America said.

Conversely, analog chip makers saw an average decline of 51 basis points quarter-over-quarter and 254 basis points year-over-year, with Onsemi (ON) and Microchip (MCHP) seeing 81 basis point declines. Analog Devices (ADI) was the only analog stock to see a sequential rise, up 6 basis points. 

AMD's rise, Synopsys' fall

Delving deeper, Bank of America said that AMD is now owned by 35% of funds and is the third-most widely owned chip stock behind Nvidia (NVDA) (at 78%) and Broadcom (AVGO) (at 74%). However, it is still underweight compared to the S&P 500, at 15th among semiconductors.

On the flip side, Synopsys (SNPS) has seen the greatest decrease in ownership, down 406 basis points quarter-over-quarter, at 26% of fund managers. However, it is still the second most overweight semiconductor stock, at 1.71x relative weight, behind KLA Corp. (KLAC) at 1.79x. Lam Research (LRCX) has seen an increase in ownership, up 670 basis points quarter-over-quarter to 33% of fund managers, now the fourth highest in the group.

Other takes

Other notable takeaways from the survey include: Teradyne (TER) seeing a 28% decrease quarter-over-quarter in its relative weight, to 0.30x from 0.47x in January 2026; ownership in Micron (MU) increasing to 25% from 18%, amid the continued rise in memory prices. However, Micron's relative weight was down 5% quarter-over-quarter to 1.24x, the lowest in over a year but still the fifth highest in semiconductors.

Other findings include Lumentum (LITE) and Skyworks Solutions (SWKS) having the most short interest (16% and 14% of the float), while Nvidia and Broadcom have the least, at roughly 1% apiece. 

Long-only investors had higher net weighting in Skyworks, Qualcomm (QCOM), NXP Semiconductors (NXPI), Broadcom, and Texas Instruments (TXN), compared to hedge funds, which were more overweight in Teradyne, Lam Research, and Onsemi. Both long-only investors and hedge funds were overweight Applied Materials, Cadence Design Systems (CDNS), and Synopsys, the survey showed.

data.title: SA analyst upgrades/downgrades: MRVL, PM, ANET, DKNG
data.description:
Bay Area Ideas upgraded Arista Networks (ANET) following valuation contraction and improved long-term prospects, while Gary Alexander moved DraftKings (DKNG) to Neutral after a significant share price decline and the launch of its new “super app.” On the downgrade side, Cash Flow Venue moved Philip Morris (PM) to Hold citing regulatory headwinds and premium valuation, and Deep Value Investing downgraded Marvell Technology (MRVL) as custom silicon growth guidance disappointed relative to AI industry leaders.

Upgrades

  • Arista Networks (ANET): Upgrade Hold to Buy by Bay Area Ideas. The analyst cites a contraction in valuation and improved long-term prospects, with robust demand evidenced by purchase commitments increasing 42% quarter-over-quarter despite near-term supply constraints.

“I would say that despite the supply constraints and softer than desired 2026 guidance, Arista Networks' positioning is quite strong for the long term.”

  • DraftKings (DKNG): Upgrade to Neutral by Gary Alexander. Following a 30%+ decline in share price, the stock’s valuation has become more attractive, with the launch of a consolidated “super app” expected to drive iGaming growth and cross-promotion efficiency in 2026.

“Amid strong recent Q4 results, I do think DraftKings is now a more balanced bag of positives and negatives, with risks outweighing opportunities. I'm upping my rating on this stock to "Neutral.”

Downgrades

  • Philip Morris International (PM): Downgrade to Hold by Cash Flow Venue. Despite leading the smoke-free movement with smoke-free products comprising 41.5% of results, the analyst cites valuation concerns and India’s ban on heated tobacco products as limiting near-term upside.

“I'm not surprised with a downward pressure on PM's stock price given recent developments on that front. Also, I feel that investors are concerned with the large valuation premium PM holds over MO, which, in my view, limits the upside potential.”

  • Marvell Technology (MRVL): Downgrade to Hold by Deep Value Investing. The analyst is concerned that custom silicon/XPU growth guidance of 20% year-over-year for FY27 looks too modest compared to stronger AI leaders like Broadcom, despite raised total revenue guidance.

“I am downgrading to a hold as I see a painful uphill battle against stronger competitors in both the XPU and optics space. I think money will be chasing growth in the semiconductor industry (once the rotation out of tech comes to an end), and the likes of Broadcom look far more compelling than Marvell from an AI growth perspective.”

data.title: SA Asks: Which energy stocks are most exposed to the Strait of Hormuz crisis?
data.description:
Which energy stocks are most exposed to a closure of the Strait of Hormuz amid the U.S.-Israel-Iran conflict?

Seeking Alpha analysts Fluidsdoc and Grassroots Trading weigh in.

Fluidsdoc: Closure of the strait is causing disruption to the movement of oil and LNG out of the Persian Gulf to markets in the west. Qatar's operations, in particular, have been impacted by this threat. Early in the conflict, it chose to shut down its LNG facilities at Ras Laffan, through which it processes and exports 77 mpta (20% of global capacity) of the product. 

Companies whose earnings statements could be impacted by this event include ConocoPhillips (COP), ExxonMobil (XOM), Shell (SHEL), and TotalEnergies (TTE). These companies own interests in various phases of projects that feed into Ras Laffan, including Qatargas 3, North Field South, and North Field Expansion. 

That said, these companies are all so huge that the investment thesis for them would not be seriously affected in the short term.

Grassroots Trading: The companies that would be most directly affected by the Strait of Hormuz closure are the ones that produce oil and export LNG from the Persian Gulf.

This is especially true for companies connected to Qatar’s LNG exports and oil/gas production in the Gulf region, because their shipments have to pass through the Strait of Hormuz to get to global markets. Examples include Shell (SHEL), TotalEnergies (TTE), and BP (BP).  Meanwhile, even if Exxon Mobil (XOM) doesn't ship all the natural gas itself, its partnership in related projects means it could still be indirectly affected as gas shipments through that region continue to face disruptions.

That said, large integrated oil companies operate in many segments of the energy business, so they're generally diversified enough to benefit as oil prices rise. In contrast, companies that focus mainly on upstream activities, such as exploration and production, and don't have refining or chemical operations to balance their businesses would likely see the sharpest earnings volatility. ConocoPhillips (COP), Occidental Petroleum (OXY), EOG Resources (EOG), Devon Energy (DVN), and APA (APA) come to mind.

data.title: Better Home & Finance targets $1B monthly loan volume by May 2026 amid Tinman AI platform expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Better Home & Finance Holding Company (BETR) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Vishal Garg, Founder & CEO, opened the call by welcoming new CFO Loveen Advani, highlighting his operational and strategic finance expertise, and emphasizing the company's transition into an AI-native mortgage platform: "Better is a vertical AI platform fundamentally reshaping and revolutionizing the home finance industry. We are building the AI native frontier of consumer finance... Adoption across the ecosystem confirms this shift is real and accelerating."
  • Garg detailed major partnerships, notably the launch with Intuit Credit Karma, which has over 140 million members, integrating Credit Karma’s data into Better’s Tinman AI platform. He described the opportunity as massive, with Credit Karma aiming to save its members $1 trillion in mortgage interest, and noted the company has penetrated less than 1% of Credit Karma’s eligible user base so far.
  • He highlighted the partnership with NEO, which scaled from a $1.5 billion to a $2.4 billion run rate in 2025, and a top 5 U.S. nonbank mortgage originator that went live with Tinman in February, with plans to expand to over 3,000 loan officers.
  • Garg announced the launch of the first conversational credit decision engine for mortgages and home equity loans directly integrated into ChatGPT via Tinman AI. He reported immediate industry interest: "Within days of releasing a short demonstration video last week, we've received inbound interest from over 40 financial institutions, mortgage companies, banks, fintechs."
  • He pointed to significant financial achievements: Q4 2025 funded loan volume of $1.5 billion and $44 million in revenue, with Tinman AI generating $646 million in volume and surpassing prior guidance. Garg stated, "We remain on track to reach $1 billion in monthly volume by May 2026 and to reach adjusted EBITDA breakeven by the end of the third quarter 2026."
  • Cost efficiency and platform advantages were emphasized, with Garg stating, "Our cost to process, underwrite and close a loan... is about $800 a loan, which is far less than anyone else in the industry."
  • Loveen Advani, CFO, described the transition to an AI-native platform as "a fundamental reshaping of our revenue mix," noting, "In 2024, our total funded volume was $3.6 billion with 0% contribution from Tinman's AI platform partnerships. In 2025... 35% coming from our Tinman AI platform. Looking ahead to 2026, we see a clear path to over 60% of our loan volume coming from our Tinman AI platform business."

Outlook

  • Advani reported, "For our total loan volume, we expect $1.4 billion to $1.55 billion in Q1 2026, of which the midpoint is a 70% year-over-year growth from Q1 '25." He reaffirmed the target of reaching $1 billion total monthly loan volume by May 2026 and adjusted EBITDA breakeven by the end of Q3 2026.
  • Management expects Tinman AI platform to drive a growing share of volume, projecting around 60% of total loan volume in 2026, up from 35% in 2025.

Financial Results

  • Q4 2025 funded loan volume reached approximately $1.5 billion, reflecting a 56% year-over-year increase, and revenue was approximately $44 million, up 77% year-over-year. Tinman AI platform generated $646 million in Q4 volume, exceeding previous guidance.
  • Product mix for Q4: 49% purchase, 37% refinance, 14% home equity; channel mix: 44% Tinman AI partners, 56% direct-to-consumer.
  • Full year 2025 funded loan volume was $4.7 billion and revenue $165 million, with 35% of loan volume via Tinman AI partnerships.
  • Q4 adjusted EBITDA loss was approximately $24 million, compared to a $25 million loss in the prior quarter.
  • Per loan contribution margin improved 28% quarter-over-quarter from approximately $1,800 to $2,300 per loan.
  • Company ended Q4 with $227 million in cash, restricted cash, short-term investments, and assets held for sale. Warehouse facilities totaled $575 million in capacity.

Q&A

  • Ramsey El-Assal, Cantor Fitzgerald: Asked about flat Q1 loan volume guidance amid company momentum. Advani responded, "It's flat because of seasonality... from Q4 '25 to our guidance of Q1 '26, it's flat or slightly up. That just shows the kind of growth in the platform."
  • El-Assal followed up on profitability. Advani prioritized reaching profitability by Q3 2026 and outlined expected contribution margin ranges for new partnerships. Garg elaborated on margin profiles across D2C, Tinman platform, and partner-closed loans.
  • Kartik Mehta, Northcoast Research: Asked about preapproval to funded loan conversion rates and stablecoin funding progress. Garg said, "It bakes to 5%" for funding from preapprovals and expects stablecoin ecosystem benefits to be realized in about six months.
  • Brendan McCarthy, Sidoti: Sought details on Credit Karma partnership and volume expectations. Garg expects, "Credit Karma Home Loans, powered by Better to be the single largest originator of mortgages in this country."
  • Eric Hagen, BTIG: Queried why cost savings aren’t more visible to borrowers. Garg replied, "We are passing the savings on our side to the borrowers... our rates are 30 basis points cheaper on average than the average mortgage rate."
  • Rohit Kulkarni, ROTH Capital: Asked about Tinman platform ramp. Advani and Garg explained the ramping process and expected Tinman share to rise from 0% in 2024 to about 60% in 2026.
  • Ryan Tomasello, KBW: Requested differentiation points for Tinman platform. Garg emphasized, "Tinman is the only platform in its class that allows the loans to be sold to a wide network of investors who can bring their own guidelines and their own pricing into the platform."
  • Owen Rickert, Northland: Asked what’s needed to double quarterly volume. Garg said, "To go from $1.5 billion to $3 billion is just penetrate the existing partners we already have signed up and implemented with."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was inquisitive and focused on ramping partnerships, profitability timelines, competitive differentiation, and cost advantages, with a neutral to slightly positive sentiment about operational progress and growth opportunities.
  • Management expressed confidence, emphasizing transformative momentum and operational leverage, supported by statements like "We remain on track to reach $1 billion in monthly volume by May 2026 and to reach adjusted EBITDA breakeven by the end of the third quarter 2026."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts’ tone shifted from cautious optimism to more direct inquiries about implementation progress, while management’s tone became more confident and forward-looking.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management language shifted from describing ramping partnerships and initial AI platform adoption in Q3 to reporting significant Tinman AI-driven volume and revenue in Q4.
  • Tinman AI platform’s share of total volume increased from 0% in 2024 to 35% in 2025, with a projected rise to 60% in 2026.
  • Q4’s focus was on exceeding Tinman platform guidance, the launch of new AI products, and onboarding additional enterprise partners. In Q3, emphasis centered on new partnership announcements and early-stage scaling.
  • Analyst questions in Q4 focused on the specifics of partnership ramp, platform contribution, and margin profile, whereas Q3 questions were broader, covering partnership pipeline and expense management.
  • Management confidence and specificity in financial and operational targets increased markedly quarter-over-quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged seasonality in quarterly volumes and the long ramp timelines for large enterprise partnerships.
  • The transition to an AI-native platform may introduce uneven progress toward breakeven, with Advani stating, "progress towards breakeven may not be linear."
  • Garg highlighted the need for deeper partner integration and the bandwidth to launch new partnerships as key operational challenges.
  • Analyst concerns included the realization of cost savings, conversion rates, and the technical integration timeline for new partners.

Final Takeaway

Management underscored a transformational quarter, advancing the company’s transition from a direct-to-consumer lender to an AI-powered platform underpinning the broader mortgage ecosystem. With robust growth in funded loan volume and revenue, substantial Tinman AI platform adoption, and multiple large-scale partnerships underway, Better Home & Finance projects reaching $1 billion in monthly loan volume by May 2026 and adjusted EBITDA breakeven by the end of Q3 2026. The company’s leadership voiced strong confidence in both the scalability of its AI platform and the sustainability of its competitive advantages as they continue to expand partner penetration and operational efficiency.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Wells Fargo shares slip for seven consecutive sessions
data.description:
Wells Fargo & Co (WFC) shares fell for seven straight sessions, as the stock was down 0.6% at $74.77 on Friday.

The Wall Street lender fell 8.4% in the preceding six sessions. The stock is down 16% over the past one month and has dropped nearly 19% so far this year.

"WFC's 2026 guidance targets 5% NII growth and mid-single-digit loan growth, yet expense growth and credit quality risks loom. Despite resolving legacy issues, WFC's efficiency ratio remains high at 65.5%, lagging peers and limiting sustainable margin improvement," pointed out a recent Seeking Alpha analysis.

Looking at Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating, WFC has a Hold rating with a score of 3.1 out of 5. The company received an F in the prospect of profitability, while it got a B in growth factor.

Turning to the Wall Street community, 16 analysts gave WFC a Buy and above rating. 11 analysts have given the stock a Hold recommendation, while none recommended Sell.

 

Seeking Alpha analysts are cautious and see the stock as a Hold.

data.title: Quant ratings roundup as fertilizer stocks rally on Middle East supply fears
data.description:
Fertilizer stocks have rallied in recent sessions as escalating Middle East tensions raise concerns about disruptions to global supplies of nitrogen-based crop nutrients.
 
Producers including CF Industries (CF), Nutrien (NTR) and Mosaic (MOS) have climbed as traders price in tighter availability of urea and ammonia, key inputs widely used in corn and other crop production.
 
The sector is particularly sensitive to geopolitical shocks because a significant share of exports originates from regions exposed to conflict. Nearly half of global urea exports and about 30% of ammonia shipments come from countries vulnerable to Middle East disruptions, raising supply concerns just as the Northern Hemisphere spring planting season begins.
 
At the same time, rising natural gas prices in Europe, a key feedstock for nitrogen fertilizer production are widening the cost advantage for U.S. producers. 
 
Against this backdrop, here are the top fertilizer stocks along with their Quant ratings and 5-day performance.
  • Intrepid Potash (IPI): Quant Rating – Strong Buy | 5-Day Perf: 3.97%
  • CF Industries (CF): Quant Rating – Hold | 5-Day Perf: 11.46%
  • Yara International ASA (YARIY): Quant Rating – Hold | 5-Day Perf: 12.65%
  • K+S Aktiengesellschaft (KPLUY): Quant Rating – Hold | 5-Day Perf: 15.70% 
  • Nutrien (NTR): Quant Rating – Hold | 5-Day Perf: 9.12%
  • Brazil Potash (GRO): Quant Rating – Hold | 5-Day Perf: 10.86%
  • Corteva (CTVA): Quant Rating – Hold | 5-Day Perf: 3.30%
  • The Scotts Miracle-Gro (SMG): Quant Rating – Hold | 5-Day Perf: -4.21%
  • The Mosaic Company (MOS): Quant Rating – Hold | 5-Day Perf: 15.75%
  • ICL Group (ICL): Quant Rating – Hold | 5-Day Perf: 2.86%
  • FMC Corp (FMC): Quant Rating – Sell | 5-Day Perf: 4.47%
  •  Bioceres Crop Solutions (BIOX): Quant Rating – Strong Sell | 5-Day Perf: 0.09%

data.title: Vistra tops growth factor grades among S&P utilities holdings
data.description:
The S&P 500 has experienced a challenging two-week period marked by escalating geopolitical tensions, sector rotation into defensives, and technical deterioration.

In light of this, below is a list of the top 10 S&P utilities holdings arranged according to their growth factor grade.

The list is topped by Vistra (VST), with a growth grade of A. Constellation Energy (CEG) follows with an A-, representing the strongest growth profiles among the top utilities holdings. Xcel Energy (XEL) and Sempra (SRE) occupy the mid-tier positions with C+ and C grades respectively.

The list also includes major utility players such as NextEra Energy (NEE) and American Electric Power Company (AEP), while Duke Energy (DUK) and Dominion Energy (D) round out the bottom of the rankings with D- grades.

The growth factor grade is a quantitative assessment used in stock analysis to evaluate a company’s growth prospects and expansion trajectory. This metric systematically analyzes multiple growth-related indicators to determine how effectively a company is scaling its business operations.

Growth factor grades are typically expressed on a letter scale (A+ through F), where higher grades indicate stronger growth characteristics relative to sector peers. An A+ grade represents exceptional growth potential, while lower grades suggest more moderate expansion or potential headwinds.

Here is the list:

  1. Vistra (VST), Growth grade: A

  2. Constellation Energy (CEG), Growth grade: A-

  3. Xcel Energy (XEL), Growth grade: C+

  4. Sempra (SRE), Growth grade: C

  5. NextEra Energy (NEE), Growth grade: C

  6. American Electric Power (AEP), Growth grade: C-

  7. Exelon (EXC), Growth grade: D

  8. The Southern Company (SO), Growth grade: D

  9. Dominion Energy (D), Growth grade: D-

  10. Duke Energy (DUK), Growth grade: D-

data.title: Linde upgraded at JPM, 'built better for current market conditions than many materials companies'
data.description:
Linde (LIN) +1.4% in Friday's trading as J.P. Morgan upgraded shares to Overweight from Neutral with a $525 price target, raised from $455, saying the world's top industrial gas supplier is "built better for current market conditions than many materials companies."

Linde’s (LIN) industrial customers in the U.S. likely will increase operating rates given the opportunity to export at higher prices and capture expanded margins, and its refinery customers also should boost operating rates, JPM analyst Jeffrey Zekauskas said, noting the 3-2-1 crack spread has widened from ~$20 to $40 as oil prices have jumped.

The oversupplied helium market, which previously was a drag on Linde's (LIN) price growth, also has tightened as QatarEnergy temporarily suspended its LNG operations, Zekauskas said, adding that very little helium business is done at spot and the shorter-term effects on the company probably are minimal.

Linde (LIN) also tends to increase prices at a faster rate in an inflationary market than it does in a non-inflationary market, Zekauskas said.

The analyst had downgraded the stock just five weeks ago, foreseeing valuation risk in a slow demand environment with pockets of price weakness, but "opportunities for volume growth in the U.S. and Europe coupled with some stabilization in the rare gas and helium market should at least offset some volume penalties in Asia and Europe and the less positive currency translation effects."
